---
title: "Intermediate Elective: Birds"
author: "Kim Wong"
date: "05-08-2026"
format: pdf
execute: 
  message: false
  warning: false 
---

# Set up

## Packages 
```{r}
#| label: packages

# general use 
library(tidyverse)

# file organization 
library(here)

# data visualization
library(lubridate)
library(showtext)
library(camcorder)
library(ggtext)
library(glue)
library(tsibble)
library(rcartocolor)
library(paletteer)

```

## Data

```{r}
#| label: data

birds <- read_csv(here("data", "birds-copy.csv"))
```


# Problems 

## 1. Explain your inspiration

I chose the UFO Sightings visualization for inspiration. I want to work with the birds dataset, and I think I could show bird counts by season with a visualization like this. When I was looking through all the options, I was looking for something that could display 4 variables (for me: time, count, season, and eBird group), and this one does that clearly. For my visualization, time and bird count will be the axes, seasons will be the colors, and each plot will be for an eBird group. I am picking eBird groups based on some of the birds I've seen at NCOS these past few weeks!

## 2. Plan your figure 

```{r}
#| label: figure-sketch

knitr::include_graphics("figure-sketch.png", error = FALSE)
```

## 3. Code your figure

```{r}
#| label: cleaning-data

# create a new object from birds
birds_clean <- birds |> 
  # filtering to only include 9 groups of choice 
  # and removing repeat observations
  filter(e_bird_group %in% c("Hummingbirds",
                             "Vultures, Hawks, and Allies",
                             "Gulls, Terns, and Skimmers",
                             "Woodpeckers",
                             "Shorebirds",
                             "Tyrant Flycatchers: Pewees, Kingbirds, and 
                             Allies",
                             "Waterfowl",
                             "Jays, Magpies, Crows, and Ravens",
                             "Thrushes"),
         repeat_observation == "No") |> 
  # changing date to only keep year
  mutate(year = year(observation_date)) |> 
  # selecting columns of interest
  select(e_bird_group, year, season, count) |> 
  # changing season column to a factor
  mutate(season = as.factor(season)) |> 
  # reordering seasons, reversing order
  mutate(season = fct_relevel(season, "Fall", "Winter", "Spring", "Summer"),
         season = fct_rev(season))
```

```{r}
#| label: plot-details

# colors
bg_col <- "#fafafa"
col_pal <- rcartocolor::carto_pal(n = 5, "Sunset")
dark_col <- "grey20"

# fonts
font_add_google("Ubuntu", "Ubuntu")
showtext_auto()

# text
st <- glue::glue("This figure shows the percent of total bird counts per 
                 season for nine eBird groups at North Campus Space. 
                 For some groups, the counts are pretty even across all 
                 seasons. For others, the count appears to be higher in 
                 <span style='color:{col_pal[1]}'>summer</span> and 
                 <span style='color:{col_pal[2]}'>spring</span>. Some have 
                 low counts in <span style='color:{col_pal[4]}'>winter</span>. 
                 <span style='color:{col_pal[5]}'>Fall</span> counts seem 
                 mostly proportional.")
tg <- paste0(
  "<span style='font-size:38pt; font-weight: bold;'>**Bird abundance by 
  season at NCOS**</span><br><br>", st,
  "<br><br>**Data**: North Campus Open Space<br><br>"
)
```



```{r}
#| label: plot
#| fig-height: 10
#| fig-width: 15

# base layer is ggplot
birds_plots <- ggplot(
  data = birds_clean,
  # x-axis is year
  # y-axis is bird count
  # fill by season
  mapping = aes(x = year, y = count, fill = season)
) +
  # proportional stacked plots to visualize counts as percents
  geom_area(position = "fill") +
  geom_text(
    # position eBird group names around middle of plots
    mapping = aes(x = 2021, y = 0.5, label = e_bird_group),
    # background color, font and size of text
    colour = bg_col,
    family = "Ubuntu",
    size = 3
  ) +
  # x-axis 
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(2017, 2025)) +
  # facet by eBird groups
  facet_wrap(~e_bird_group, nrow = 3, ncol = 3) +
  # color seasons
  scale_fill_carto_d(palette = "Sunset") +
  # blank axes labels and tag
  labs(
    x = "",
    y = "",
    tag = tg
  ) +
  # different theme from default
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12, base_family = "Ubuntu") +
  theme(
    # remove legend
    legend.position = "none",
    # background and borders of entire plot
    plot.background = element_rect(
      fill = bg_col,
      colour = bg_col
    ),
    # background and borders of each panel 
    panel.background = element_rect(
      fill = bg_col,
      colour = bg_col
    ),
    # tag 
    plot.tag = element_textbox_simple(
      lineheight = 0.5,
      colour = dark_col,
      margin = margin(b = 60),
      maxwidth = 0.2,
      halign = 0,
      size = 18
    ),
    # position of tag
    plot.tag.position = c(-0.1, 0.5),
    # blank panel grid instead of lines
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    # remove y-axis
    axis.text.y = element_blank(),
    # remove labels on top of plots
    strip.text = element_blank(),
    # space between panels
    panel.spacing = unit(0, "lines"),
    # margins around entire plot
    plot.margin = margin(10, 15, 10, 200),
  )

# displaying visualization
birds_plots

# saving visualization as png
ggsave("birds_plots.png", plot = birds_plots, device = "png", path = "/Users/kimberlywong/Desktop/git/intermediate-elective/code", width = 15, height = 10)
```

## 4. Describe your process

I thought that this process was a fun challenge because it gave me the freedom to choose what data to use and how to visualize it. It's cool that you can use someone else's code to create a similar visualization as them, and I liked that I still had to go through the code to figure out what each step means and how to modify it for my assignment.

I had to use the Help pane to learn about a lot of the functions I had never seen before in order to use them properly for my visualization. It was a lot of going back and forth, but was very satisfying at the end. 

Although we have previously visualized bird counts across seasons, we haven't looked at the percent of counts for each season and how that changes from 2017 to 2025. In addition to a longer time period, I also chose different eBird groups based on birds I've observed recently at NCOS. This visualization also uses labels and axes differently compared to more traditional axis labels and titles that we've used before.
